speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jung, you were telling us about your work in Deblin iron working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we were selected for work in the airport, what was a military airport, for the German army. And we worked there all kinds of work. I was selected to, like, working for iron works and construction. So what we did, all kinds of iron production and also repair of the hangars what were from metal. So we worked there, and we were treated very good in comparison to other camps. There was a camp with-- with no guard. They had-- our own people were the guards there, only we couldn't get out. Where if we were to get out, they would catch us and-- and shoot us on the spot. So. And from there, camp, we went every morning, marched to the airport. And we worked there. And we were treated, in comparison, very good. We didn't have any SS. There was only the German military and they call it Bau Company-- what means building company, construction company-- from the German army. We worked together, and we worked also with Polish people from outside. So we could somehow trade, and we could all kinds of goodies get from-- from the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough food at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, we had-- well, nobody had [CHUCKLES] nobody had enough food. Only there was always some black market in Poland, and the Polish people had the rations, and they had stamps. Only on their farms, they were raising some hogs or cattle, or a little bit of milk. So always was a little bit extra food or bread. Then we made the contact with somebody who run-- was a runner, like a courier, to Czechoslovakia, to Slovakia, to my town. And we had some money with the people there. And they were bringing us some money so we could negotiate and-- and buy some goodies, like extra milk, what we didn't get in the camp. We got soup and bread. So this was about our staple, a little bit of soup and bread. From beginning, we also got from the Kommandantur, this means from the German management, from the army, from the beginning, we got a little bit of margarine and-- and I don't know, soap or something like that. And we had some bathing facility, so we could keep clean and-- and be free of any-- any parasites or-- or so. So we were-- we were pretty lucky. There was-- we worked very hard. You work seven days a week, and the shifts were like 12 hours a day. And only we were treated fairly-- fairly good. Then later on, they eliminated the ghetto, and that was a nightmare. While we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a moment before we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go forward. And can you tell us a little bit about the living conditions with your family in the ghetto at Deblin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I told you, we were horrified to coming from our house to a strange place, what was shocking. We were-- we were not used to it. The whole life was like 50 years, 70 years back. And they brought us to a-- a house what we-- we had to share with a few families together. This means there were like two rooms and three families with kids have to share together. So some of them sleep on beds, some of them on the floors. And there was no mattress. There was a straw filled bag, and you sleep on that. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you slept?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somewhere on the floor in the corner. [LAUGHS] And I had a little brother and grandmother. And-- and I was with my mother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father, who was the grandpa, and the grandma from my mother's side, my little brother, my father, and my mother. And the-- the conditions were terrible. Well, the Germans came, and they took people. And they had a good mood, so they shoot them, or whatever they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I witnessed that. And then later on, there was the elimination of the-- of the ghetto. This time, I already didn't live home in ghetto. I lived by-- in the camp-- in the camp, by the airport. And we had-- we could-- I think so, visit once a week, opportunity to visit the grandparents and parents in ghetto. I visited them several times. And then came to end where they eliminated ghetto. And at the same time, they reduced the people in the camp where I was. They just kept probably one third, or maybe a little bit more than one third of the people in the camp where I was. They-- they called-- they made a big appell, they called it, and called out names. And they pushed us in one room. And we-- and they said, make room, make room. Well, how much room we can make, we can save more our friends. So actually, we were stacked up, and we were almost choking. They were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] putting us under, over their heads, one on the other. And the rest of them, they came, SS, and took them away. And they load them in cattle cars. And what they didn't go in cattle cars, also the people from ghetto, they were all killed. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I survived in the selection with my father, only in ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] other family had been shot, my brother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody. They were just killed. My brother was one year old. My mother and grandfather and grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this was the episode where I lost part of my family, what I knew for sure they had been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with one of our friends who was a German soldier. And we-- we-- we saw the people lying there on the-- all over, and killed. And they had been probably picked up and then bulldozed and buried. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In-- we worked on the airport for other-- over a year. And then we were put up in cars, in cattle cars. That was when the Russian front started to-- to advance, and the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germans were backing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our camp was situated between two rivers. There was [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was the other camp of Russian-- Russian prisoners of war. And one night, we wake up. We hear screaming and shooting. And there were a few hundred, maybe over one thousand, couple thousand, I didn't know where we were in. So they-- they were all killed off. They-- they eliminated them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were more lucky. We were put in cattle cars, and we were brought to Czestochowa. This is in Poland. It's a very well-known city. There was industry, and they had also the miracle of Black Madonna there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all this time, you were with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was all the time with-- with my father. And by the-- all confusion, going back, I-- I had some contact with people-- Polish people, non Jews-- and they-- they wanted to hide us. And I told my father, I says, let's go and run away while there was a confusion, till the Germans came and loaded us up, there was a confusion there. Lots of people broke out of the camp, and they ran towards the river there, where they knew the Russians were coming and crossing the river. And my father was somehow fearful, didn't want to run away, so we were loaded in the-- in the-- in the cattle cars. And there was also a terrible experience while we traveled. Now I cannot remember, maybe two days in cattle cars. Again, just standing room. And brought-- some people died. And you-- they were there, and getting heart attack. And you just were locked up. And-- and we came to Czestochowa. And again, there was three different factories they choose us to work. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about when the train and the cattle cars arrived at Czestochowa. What did you see when the-- when the doors were finally opened after those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they screamed, get out, get out, get out. And they brought us to a place. And then they-- they choose. They said, we need people for work in different factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I choose to go to work-- well, they asked, we have iron work, we have this and that. So I said, I choose to go to work to-- to iron work. There was the iron steel work, like production of iron. So I chose to work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very lucky. I was chosen to work on steam engines. Work around the-- what they-- around the-- what's work, how you call it, rolling mills. Where the iron comes in ingot, out from the oven and run through the roll-- rolling mills. And they make railroad-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no-- all kinds of iron work or wire, or-- or anything. So I worked inside of-- on the machines. We were running those rolling mills. And I got befriended with people who were also from outside, Polish people. While I learned in between the Polish language pretty fluent. And sometimes I ran the shifts for them. They didn't show up. They told me, I don't show up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, shift was a 12 hour shift, seven days a week. So I-- I ran-- I ran his shift, and he brought me a piece of bacon and a piece of bread. And this was heaven on Earth to-- to receive a piece of bread and a piece of bacon. And that was like raw bacon, and you just slice it and eat it with the bread like-- like a delicacy. So I-- I developed a very good relation with with-- with the people who worked there, with the machinist. And then I ran different kind of machinery in-- in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father worked like a foreman. He-- he was a big, strong man, and he spoke fluent German, and so forth. Well, he came from a region where they spoke German, when there was Austria and Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was, most time, foreman. And-- and he had a good job. And I had a good job. So we were pretty still strong in comparing to. We had all the chances to survive the war, until again, the front advanced. And-- Let's stop for a minute and go back, still, to your living conditions, then, at Czestochowa. Were you in-- were you with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my father in a camp. And we-- the sleeping conditions there were very bad. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a barrack with, I don't know, there were like very, very narrow-- narrow quarters, and cold. You know, in winter, it's below zero. And you had only one blanket there. And over there, the-- the conditions were bad, where you had-- if-- if you could sleep, then the bed bugs would-- would eat you alive. So what the Germans didn't take your blood, the bed bugs would take the blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when-- so sometime, I was lucky enough, I said I had a shift to run or repairs, and so I stayed in the machine room, where it was warm from the hot iron, you see. The red hot iron come in big box and going through. And so I slept-- I made myself a bed on a piece of board about so big between two pipes where the steam was coming through the big steam engines. And-- and I slept, and I knew the bed bugs wouldn't be, and I could sleep warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, every time an ingot-- this means that ingot, big like this chair-- would go in-- in the rolling mills, every time [CHUCKLES] they shake-- shake the board with you on it, there was nice, warm, cozy, and the bed bugs would not eat you. So I was pretty lucky. Some winter times I could sneak over and sleep over, what was very unusual to-- to let you do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, what-- what was your clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing was tough. Just what I wear on myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a prison uniform at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have the-- the prison uniform. I had all kinds of-- we-- we got from the Germans. And shoes, what-- what we could, what we got. And that was very bad. Well, I worked a lot with oil, the-- the most steam engines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the shift was-- was-- oil soaked in clothing. Matter of fact, sometimes you get, from the oil, like small infections and so. So actually, through the shift, before the shift was through, I could put the cloth through the pipes, what was real hot steam, and could wash-- wash it through, through steam. And then quick dry it, and there was no problem with that. And what-- Resourceful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And sometimes, I got some raw potatoes when they brought me, the Polish people who worked with me, the mechanics. And today, it reminds me when I pass [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by in my kitchen, the red element. I used to cook-- when they cut a piece of iron off of those railroad whatever, or iron, so I put it on this piece of iron, what was cut off, and I could cook my potatoes on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the condition there was still, in comparison to other-- to other people and other Jewish people there was extremely good. While in some camps, I mean, there was destruction and no food at all until you drop. Did you have any friends from Presov--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had still people who were with us when they came from Deblin with us in-- in this camp. Then when the-- when the Russian front advanced again, we were-- we knew this-- we know already this-- we will have to evacuate. Well, we heard news. We were in contact with outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you in contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the people I worked, with the Polish people who we worked on the rolling mills in there, production. What they did, they did from iron ore. They brought it to high stove, they melt it, they made iron, steel, and so forth. So there were also Polish people who worked, and they went home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear from them about the outside news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were already also some who were in underground, and there were partisans. Matter of fact, there was one time when they told us we have to stop the production of-- of iron. And what they did-- what they didn't want the production for the Germans to have iron-- so what they did-- they brought cooler iron into the-- to the rolling mills, and they told us, put full steam. Where they-- where they put the higher RPMs on the machine. And here, they put the-- the iron in, and here there was an explosion. Well, between the steam engine and the rolling mills is a big coupling, what is like, I don't know, like-- like this table or several chairs. And they just tore it apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans, right away, came with the thing. What's happened? Sabotage, sabotage. We will kill you all, and-- and so forth. Only, we had not much to lose, not the Polish people, and we, either. So-- What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They simmered down, and for several days, we-- the production had to stop. So that was a big disadvantage for the Germans, where the iron was very essential for their economy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the front [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] advanced, we-- we had some news this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we'd be evacuated. So one fellow particularly, who I was befriended from Polish people, he says--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name was Vladek Pavelec. He was a slim, tall guy. And he got to like me. And he says-- in Polish, he says, why don't you come with me. Tell your father. Last day. He told me where he will wait for me and we will escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told my father, and my father, he says, you know, I don't know. What-- while-- if the front comes back, they catch you. And if they catch him, they would kill him, too. And so there was a big-- but he-- he was ready to-- to-- to risk his life and take us, only my father somehow didn't-- didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later on, we were gathered. And-- and we were taken to a place, and I was sure they will eliminate us while we were waiting for transportation for railroad cars, and they didn't arrive. So they took us to a place, there was like a gully, and they were-- the Germans-- gathered up. And I was sure that they will kill us all there. And then somehow, we waited there like almost over a half a day. All of a sudden, they say the railroad cars arrived. So they packed us in the railroad cars, and we traveled for several days. And we arrived to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again in cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: In cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was with my father. And what happened, I made myself a dish with-- aluminum dish, and I cooked up beans. There were like this big beans. I don't know what you call that. They were like-- we called it [INAUDIBLE] beans. [INAUDIBLE] beans. So I-- we somehow rationed, and there was about yea big around. And we-- we ate this, just-- well, we didn't know how long. Water, we didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the train journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You didn't get nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you at least had that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had that. And we arrived to Buchenwald. And over there, there was already SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many days it took you to go from Czestochowa to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several days.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Poland into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And Buchenwald is located near Leipzig. Near [NON-ENGLISH] is a very well known-- very well known-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] place for optics. And-- and this, where we arrived in Buchenwald, where again, we've been taken out of the wagons and chased. And over there, there was already beaten and dogs and everything. And when we arrived through the-- through the gate, there was a sign from iron-- 'Arbeit macht Frei.' This means labor or work makes you free, only actually, the work, you do that and with no food. So they-- they free you probably from your life, not from your spirit. They break your spirit, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at night or during the day? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. I think that it was during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us exactly what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we arrived there, and-- and we were gathered to facilities [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they took all the clothes away from you, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and shave your head. So first they-- when we arrived, and there were their prisoners standing on benches, like the sheared sheep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were-- after-- before the showers, they tried to cut your hair. And I-- we-- we already knew their trick. Like in Auschwitz, they'd take you to showers, only instead of water, there would come in gas. So if you ask, they-- they are eliminating us, or this is just to-- to shave the hair and give a shower? And so he says, no, we don't have-- we don't have gas chambers.
